item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it could not be sourced and thus is to be assumed as non-compliant. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find it weird that no interview log is included in the article about SCP - feels a little incomplete.
An interview could provide insight about this SCP and Jupiter - you could at least mention that he's trying to avoid subjects related to Jupiter if you don't want to write one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: but what does all of this MEAN? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bingaroo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The gas within SCP-1977-A turned silvery gray and began giving off electrical sparks.

Metallic hydrogen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All fixed :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gotcha, that makes sense. Maybe PM the author and see if they'll consider adding the idea in?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nope, haven't read that, but like I said I have worked in the field and talked to people who were deaf themselves and worked with deaf children full time.

Talking to it would be as simple as using a whiteboard, perhaps with a note that scientific concepts can be in English while conversation speech should be in Portugese. It talking to you would involve writing the best guess from lip reading on the white board and getting confirmation (eye blinks?) on every word. If you wanted to get more technical you could include a code book where longer words or scientific concepts were communicated using simpler words. Like "code 1 5"; "code" being the delineator, "1" meaning planet and the following "5" meaning Jupiter. Details would probably clog up the article so mentioning the white board and the code book should be enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Urban legend.

Have you by any chance read Hands of My Father by Myron Uhlberg? Granted, that's the extent of my memory regarding the mention of use of Deaf individuals as spies.

This is compounded when you're talking to a scientist in two languages; there's no way they're going to be able to have a conversation back and forth on lip reading.

Do you have a suggestion regarding a better procedure?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Urban legend. I've actually worked with deaf children and deaf adults, including deaf adults who have worked in education and done research into this field. Like I said, there is a small success rate, but not enough to get full meanings. This is compounded when you're talking to a scientist in two languages; there's no way they're going to be able to have a conversation back and forth on lip reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why do you think pitchers cover their mouths with their gloves when they meet with their catchers on the mound?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Lip reading" is not actually something people can do.

Not necessarily true. Granted, just because something can be done doesn't mean it can be done well all the time, or taught.

Perhaps refer to this as a simple resource. Having taken some ASL classes in my lifetime, I can tell you that there were instances of Deaf people being used as spies because they were rather proficient at lip reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Lip reading" is not actually something people can do. A large part of speech is tongue and throat movements that are not visible from the outside. At best you pick up about 20% of what was said. If you want to communicate effectively there has to be some sort of writing involved. It would be easy talking to it, but getting its responses back would require a decoding process.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man I dunno if I'm just that right amount of stoned to understand this or it's just plain awesome. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like the article. What makes me the most interested for some reason is the second Addendum. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm quite fond of this. I like the idea that getting all the jars in the right place and figuring out how to communicate must have taken the Foundation forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, this is awesome. The scale of this guy's transformation really makes it. He's not just a floating eye and a jar full of gas, he's a floating eye and 169 jars full of gas. The mechanism necessary for him to be able to communicate is probably my favorite part of the whole thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i usually just stare at this until i become aware of my pupils dilating (warning lots of flashing lights)

thank you :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the implication, although I strongly doubt Jupiter's atmosphere would appear reddish at all in such a low quantity. I feel there needs to be a bit more to this for me to upvote. It's a good start, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Righty-oh, then.

Upvoted straightaway!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure thing chief :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
first, SCP-XXXX -> SCP-1977. I'll upvote this when you fix that. this is really goddamn weird.

second, can you PM me your dealer's number cause you're smoking some gooooooood shit man. i don't care how far i have to drive

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fix the SCP-XXXX, please.
